paper_title: Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) significantly enhances large language models (LLMs) reasoning but severely suffers from calibration degeneration, where models become excessively over-confident in incorrect answers. Previous studies devote to directly incorporating calibration objective into existing optimization target. However, our theoretical analysis demonstrates that there exists a fundamental gradient conflict between the optimization for maximizing policy accuracy and minimizing calibration error. Building on this insight, we propose DCPO, a simple yet effective framework that systematically decouples reasoning and calibration objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DCPO not only preserves accuracy on par with GRPO but also achieves the best calibration performance and substantially mitigates the over-confidence issue. Our study provides valuable insights and practical solution for more reliable LLM deployment.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards Zhengzhao Ma 1 2 Xueru Wen 1 2 Boxi Cao 1 Yaojie Lu 1 Hongyu Lin 1 Jinglin Yang 3 4 5 Min He 5 Xianpei Han 1 2 Le Sun 1 2 6 2 0 2 0 1 ] . [ 1 7 1 1 9 0 . 3 0 6 2 : r Abstract Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) significantly enhances large language models (LLMs) reasoning but severely suffers from calibration degeneration, where models become excessively over-confident in incorrect answers. Previous studies devote to directly incorporating calibration objective into existing optimization target. However, our theoretical analysis demonstrates that there exists fundamental gradient conflict between the optimization for maximizing policy accuracy and minimizing calibration error. Building on this insight, we propose DCPO, simple yet effective framework that systematically decouples reasoning and calibration objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DCPO not only preserves accuracy on par with GRPO but also achieves the best calibration performance and substantially mitigates the over-confidence issue. Our study provides valuable insights and practical solution for more reliable LLM deployment. 1. Introduction Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (Lambert et al., 2024) have recently emerged as cornerstone for shaping the remarkable reasoning abilities of large language models (Guo et al., 2025; Jaech et al., 2024). By optimizing policies using automatically verifiable rewards with on-policy sampling, representative RLVR algorithms such as GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) has achieved remarkable advances in mathematical reasoning (Guo et al., 2025; Hu 1Chinese Information Processing Laboratory, Institute of Software, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China 2University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China 3Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100085, China 4School of Cyber Security, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100085, China 5National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team/Coordination Center of China, Beijing 100029, China. Preprint. March 11, 2026. 1 Figure 1. Illustration of gradient conflict between policy accuracy maximization and calibration error minimization. et al., 2025), code generation (Luo et al., 2025a), and question answering (Jaech et al., 2024; Hu et al., 2025) tasks. Despite the success, RLVR often leads to severe calibration degeneration, emerging as critical bottleneck that limits the practical applicability of LLMs in real-world scenarios (Bereket & Leskovec, 2025; Chhikara, 2025; Kirichenko et al., 2025). In particular, RL-trained models often become significantly over-confident, assigning extremely high probability mass to their outputs even when the answers are incorrect. In high-stakes domains such as healthcare, law, and finance, such over-confidence on wrong outputs can mislead users about the systems reliability and uncertainty, resulting in inappropriate decisions and amplified systemic risk. (Yao et al., 2025; Omar et al., 2024) To address this challenge, previous studies (Damani et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2024; Leng et al., 2024) attempt to achieve coupled optimization of correctness and calibration by incorporating calibration objectives into the existing RL optimization targets. Unfortunately, empirical experiments demonstrate that such paradigm leads to accuracy-calibration tradeoff, where improvements in calibration frequently come at the expense of reasoning accuracy. Therefore, how to reliably improve the calibration of LLMs without compromising their reasoning capability has become an urgent challenge for ensuring their trustworthy deployment in real-world applications. To investigate the underlying causes of the above issues in RLVR, we conduct series of theoretical analyses1 of the prevailing optimization paradigms, thereby uncovering the key factors that give rise to the accuracycalibration 1A concise theoretical analysis is provided in Section 4, with the complete mathematical derivation deferred to the appendix A. Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards Figure 2. The overall framework of DCPO, which leverages block-wise verbalized confidence rollout and decoupled advantage estimation to decouple the optimization objectives of accuracy and calibration, and further integrates instance-level and group-level signals for more stable calibration optimization. tradeoff. Specifically, we reveal critical gradient conflict between accuracy and calibration. As illustrated in Figure 1, for over-confident models trained with RLVR, the gradient direction for maximizing accuracy is negatively aligned with the gradient direction for minimizing calibration error. That is, the Fisher-metric inner product between these two gradients is negative, implying direct optimization conflict. This finding sheds light on why previous approaches are plagued by the accuracycalibration tradeoff, since naively coupling these objectives struggles to reach Pareto-optimal state for either objective. Moreover, we find that traditional instance-level binary supervision is insufficient for calibration optimization in RLVR, as it is highly stochastic, exhibits high variance, and drives the model toward overly sharp probability distributions. The above analysis indicates that the key to jointly optimizing accuracy and calibration in RLVR lies in decoupling the optimization gradients and introducing more informative supervision signals. Building on the above insights, we propose Decoupled Calibration Policy Optimization(DCPO), simple yet effective framework that systematically decouples reasoning accuracy and calibration objectives at the levels of generation structure, reward design, and gradient optimization. Specifically, as demonstrated in Figure 2, DCPO requires the model to explicitly verbalize its confidence after reasoning trajectory generation. We assign separate rewards to the reasoning tokens and the confidence tokens, and apply masked gradient strategy to ensure that the two parts of the sequence are optimized toward distinct objectives. In this way, DCPO fundamentally avoids the accuracycalibration gradient conflict and enables parallel improvement of both reasoning ability and confidence reliability. Furthermore, to construct stable and low-variance supervision for calibration optimization, we exploit the group sampling mechanism inherent in GRPO (Shao et al., 2024). Specifically, we prove that, although the correctness of individual instances is not precisely distinguished, the average correctness within rollout group provides more stable estimate of the models uncertainty for given input. Accordingly, we supervise confidence prediction using both instance-level and grouplevel accuracy, without requiring any additional annotation or external oracle. This joint signal yields consistent and low-variance calibration feedback during training. To comprehensively verify the effectiveness of DCPO, we conduct experiments on 5 widely-used mathematical reasoning benchmarks spanning wide range of difficulty levels (e.g., MATH (Hendrycks et al., 2021), AIME (Zhang & Math-AI, 2024; 2025), AMC (Yang et al., 2024a)). The results demonstrate that, compared with previous baselines, DCPO achieves the best tradeoff between reasoning performance and calibration. Specifically, while previous coupled optimization methods significantly reduce model accuracy compared to vanilla GRPO, our DCPO not only preserves accuracy on par with GRPO but also achieves the best calibration performance and substantially mitigates the overconfidence issue. For instance, QWEN3-8B trained with DCPO achieves an average accuracy improvement of 11.8% across 5 benchmarks, matching the performance of vanilla GRPO and outperforming RLCR by 4.3% and CCGPSG by 3.2%. Meanwhile,DCPO achieves relative Expected Calibration Error (ECE) reduction of 71.6% compared with QWEN3-8B, decreasing it from 0.435 to 0.128. Our main contributions are summarized as follows2 We identify fundamental mechanism underlying calibration degeneration in RLVR and formalize the gradient conflict between accuracy and calibration. We introduce DCPO, simple yet effective framework that systematically decouples reasoning and calibration objectives. Experiments demonstrate that DCPO achieves the best tradeoff between reasoning performance and calibration compared with previous strong baselines. 2The code is available at https://github.com/ icip-cas/DCPO. 2 Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards Figure 3. Reliability diagrams for different LLMs. The dashed line denotes perfect calibration; bar height indicates empirical accuracy per confidence bin, and color intensity reflects sample frequency. The Expected Calibration Error (ECE) is reported above each subplot, revealing prevalent over-confidence across models. 2. Preliminaries and Related Work 2.3. Calibration Estimation This section first introduces widely used RLVR algorithm, Group Relative Policy Optimization (Shao et al., 2024), and then reviews the commonly used methods for measuring LLM confidence and calibration. Calibration measures the alignment between predicted confidence and empirical correctness (Guo et al., 2017). commonly used metric is Expected Calibration Error (ECE) (DeGroot & Fienberg, 1983), defined as 2.1. Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) is an outcome-based RLVR algorithm that normalize rewards within sampled response group. Given prompt q, the policy πθ samples group of responses {oi}G i=1, each assigned scalar reward ri. GRPO computes group-relative advantage Ai = ri σ , = 1 (cid:88) i=1 ri, σ2 = 1 G (cid:88) (ri m)2. (1) i=1 Group normalization reduces reward scale sensitivity and provides low-variance signal, making GRPO widely adopted in recent RLVR pipelines. ECE = (cid:88) m=1 Bm (cid:12)a(Bm) c(Bm)(cid:12) (cid:12) (cid:12), (2) where Bm denotes confidence bin with average confidence c(Bm) and accuracy a(Bm). AUROC (Hanley & McNeil, 1982) is commomly used to measure the discriminative quality of confidence scores. During reinforcement learning, ECE may decrease trivially as accuracy improves. To better characterize over-confidence, we additionally report Positive Calibration Error (PCE), which restricts ECE to bins where confidence exceeds accuracy. Formally, PCE is defined as: PCE(c, r) = (cid:88) c(Bm)>a(Bm) Bm a(Bm) c(Bm) . (3) 2.2. Confidence Estimation 2.4. Calibration Optimization Methods Model confidence measures language modes self-assessed probablity that its generated answer is correct (Yoon et al., 2025). Existing approaches can be categorized into 3 classes (Moskvoretskii et al., 2025; Heo et al., 2024). Tokenbased confidence derives confidence from internal generation statistics, such as token probabilities (Fomicheva et al., 2020; Kadavath et al., 2022). While effective, it often requires additional computation and lacks interpretability. In this work, we adopt common practice and estimate tokenbased confidence using the sequence probability (Zheng et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2025; Fomicheva et al., 2020; Grabinski et al., 2022): Conf(y) = (cid:81)y i=1 πθ(yi q, y<i). Verbalized confidence prompts models to explicitly output confidence score alongside the answer (Lin et al., 2022; Xiong et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024b), offering flexible and human-interpretable interface (Yoon et al., 2025). Consistency-based confidence estimates uncertainty via agreement among multiple sampled outputs (Lin et al., 2023; Ding et al., 2024), which is often considered to excel in downstream performance at the cost of additional sampling (Moskvoretskii et al., 2025). Existing calibration optimization methods fall into 2 categories. Post-hoc and inference-time methods (Wang et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025) optimize calibration independently of training, e.g., inference-time distractors (Chhikara, 2025) or external confidence predictors (Ni et al., 2025). Calibration-aware RL methods (Zhou et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2024) integrate uncertainty objectives into policy optimization. For instance, CCGPSG (Liu et al., 2025) modifies GRPO objective according to token-based confidence, and RLCR (Damani et al., 2025) incorparates Brier Score loss into RLVR rewards. These methods improve calibration but often entangle confidence learning with correctness optimization, leading to accuracycalibration tradeoffs. 3. Empirical Analysis for Calibration

sections.title: Degeneration
sections.content: In this section, we find that current LLMs consistently suffer from the over-confidence issue, and this over-confidence issue is further exacerbated by RLVR. Furthermore, we demonstrate that existing coupled optimization approaches 3 Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards Figure 4. The impact of RLVR on LLM calibration, which demonstrate that model confidence increases during RLVR training and RLVR exacerbates the models over-confidence. cannot resolve this issue without noticeably degrading model performance. 3.1. Over-Confidence of Current LLMs To systematically quantify the calibration behavior of existing LLMs, we conduct comprehensive evaluation across different model families and scales on suite of mathematical reasoning benchmarks. Figure 3 reports the reliability diagrams of these LLMs on the combined AMC23 and AMC24 datasets. The results demonstrate mis-calibration is pervasive across all evaluated base models and is primarily driven by systematic over-confidence. Specifically, all models exhibit large ECE, with values consistently exceeding 0.3, indicating severe deviation from ideal calibration. Moreover, across most confidence bins, the empirical accuracy bars lie substantially below the diagonal, showing that models frequently assign high confidence to incorrect answers. 3.2. RLVR Leads to Calibration Degradation To systematically examine how reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) affects model calibration, we conduct GRPO training on Qwen3-8B (non-thinking) (Yang et al., 2025). The model is trained on the DeepScaler dataset (Luo et al., 2025b), and calibration performance is evaluated on AIME24 using 8 repeated samplings to obtain stable estimates. Figure 4 summarizes the evolution of model confidence and over-confidence during RLVR training. Figure 4(a) tracks the average predicted confidence over training steps and demonstrates clear confidence upward trend. In particular, GRPO steadily increases the models average confidence from about 0.88 to above 0.98, and decrease model confidence variance from 0.006 to about 0.001. Figure 4(b) further quantifies this phenomenon using PCE, which isolates substantial amplification of over-confidence. In particular, after GRPO training, PCE increases from 0.312 to 0.362. Figure 5. The accuracy and calibration performance of QWEN38B trained with different RL methods.The figures illustrate that while existing calibration optimization methods can improve model calibration, their accuracy decreases. Together, these results show that, although RLVR improves reasoning accuracy, it simultaneously degrades calibration by pushing the model toward excessively confident predictions, even when answers are incorrect. This empirical evidence highlights fundamental limitation of correctnessonly RL optimization and motivates the need for calibrationaware training strategies that explicitly control confidence during reinforcement learning. 3.3. Accuracy-Calibration Tradeoff of Coupled Optimization Recent calibration-aware reinforcement learning methods aim to jointly optimize reasoning accuracy and confidence calibration by directly incorporating calibration-related objectives into the RL reward or advantage function. To study the impact of coupled optimization objectives on model performance and calibration, we compare standard GRPO with two representative coupled calibration methods, RLCR (Damani et al., 2025) and CCGSPG (Liu et al., 2025), on the AIME24 benchmark. For each method, we evaluate reasoning accuracy together with calibration metrics, including ECE and PCE, enabling systematic analysis of the accuracycalibration relationship under coupled optimization. Figure 5 summarizes the results, which indicates that such coupled optimization fundamentally impact reasoning performance. In particular, both RLCR and CCGSPG substantially reduce ECE and PCE compared to GRPO, indicating improved calibration, while both methods exhibit noticeable reasoning accuracy performance drop relative to GRPO. This suggests that directly coupling calibration objectives with correctness-driven optimization introduces gradient interference, where enforcing conservative confidence estimates suppresses the learning signal for correct reasoning. 4. Theoretical Analysis The experimental results in Section 3 imply that RLVR method introduces severe over-confidence problem and existing coupled calibration optimization methods exhibit Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards accuracycalibration tradeoff. To dive into the underlying causes of the above issues, we firstly analyze why current RLVR exacerbates over-confidence. Furthermore, we reveal an accuracy-calibration gradient conflict, which explains why previous coupled optimization are plagued by accuracycalibration tradeoff. Finally, we show that group-level accuracy can serve as low-variance supervision signal for calibration training, inspiring the design of the calibration reward in DCPO. Collectively, these theoretical analyses provide solid foundation for the design of our DCPO algorithm. We present concise proofs in this section and detailed proofs in Appendix A. 4.1. Trajectory-Level Reinforcement Learning Induces Over-Confidence Findings 1. The over-confidence phenomenon is structural consequence of trajectory-level RLVR. For fixed input x, let πθ(y x) denote the probability of generating complete response trajectory y. Let R(y) {0, 1} be trajectory-level correctness indicator and define + := {y R(y) = 1} (4) The expected accuracy objective optimized by trajectorylevel reinforcement learning is Jacc(θ) := Eyπθ (x)[R(y)] = (cid:88) yY+ πθ(y x) (5) In the absence of Proposition 4.1 (Mode Collapse). explicit entropy regularization, any optimal solution to maxθ Jacc(θ) assigns probability mass 1 to single trajectory +. Proof. Let (Y) denote the probability simplex over the finite or countable set of all possible trajectories Y. Then find the optimizal solution to objective Jacc(θ) is equivalent to solve p(y). (6) max p(Y) (cid:88) yY+ Obviously, objective 6 is linear programming problem on (Y), which is convex polygonal set of p. By the fundamental theorem of linear programming, the optimal solution to this question is approached on extreme point δy , where δy (y) = I[y = y]. As R(y) {0, 1}, we must have R(y) = 1 at the optimal solution. detailed proof is provided in Appendix A.1 We further note that these low-entropy solutions are stable under small perturbations of the input. Since the policy logits are continuous functions of x, extreme logit margins at training inputs induce neighborhoods in input space where πθ(y x) 1 max (7) As correctness is discontinuous with respect to the input, this leads to over-confident yet incorrect predictions under distribution shift. 4.2. Accuracy-Calibration Gradient Conflict Findings 2. The gradient direction for maximizing accuracy is negatively aligned with the gradient direction for minimizing calibration error.

sections.content: V ar(R(y)). (17) 5.2. Decoupled Advantage Estimation To optimize model calibration independently of accuracy, we design decoupled advantage estimators for reasoning and calibration. For the reasoning component, we employ standard outcome-based reward defined by accuracy: R(or) = I(ypred ylabel), (20) where ypred is extracted from or. detailed proof is provided in Appendix A.4 Proposition 4.4. When using the absolute calibration loss ℓ(c, r) = r, using RG as supervision for calibration reduces gradient variance compared to instance-level correctness. Proof. We consider R(y) as i.i.d Bernoulli random variable with expectation = RG. Let g(c, r) denote an arbitrary measurable selection from ccr. For any such selecetion, we have g(c, r) 1. When (0, 1) and = RG, we have: ar(g(c, R(y))) = 4p(p 1). ar(g(c, RG)) = 0. (19) Thus, ar(g(c, R(y))) ar(g(c, RG)), and the equality is approached iff {0, 1}. (18) detailed proof is provided in Appendix A.4 5. Decoupled Calibration Policy Optimization To address the correct-calibration tradeoff induced by RLVR, based on the above analysis, we propose DCPO (Decoupled Calibration Policy Optimization), decoupled reward modeling framework for calibrated training of LLMs. As shown in Figure 2, our approach separates the optimization of reasoning quality and confidence estimation by assigning distinct rewards and advantages to different segments of the model output. To provide low-variance supervision signal for confidence calibration, we leverage the group sampling mechanism in GRPO, enabling stable and effective training without sacrificing task performance. 5.1. Block-wise Verbalized Confidence Rollout To explicitly model uncertainty during reinforcement learning, we adopt block-wise verbalized confidence rollout that separates the model output into reasoning block and confidence block. Specifically, given an input q, we prompt the model to generate response in the following structured form = [ or <conf> oc ], where or contains the reasoning process and final answer, and oc is scalar confidence prediction, with the two segments separated by special delimiter token <conf>. 6 Based on the analysis in 4.3, we exploit the group sampling mechanism inherent in GRPO and introduce group-level accuracy RG = 1 i=1 R(or,i) as calibration target. (cid:80)G To balance stability and expressiveness, we introduce hybrid calibration target that interpolates between grouplevel and instance-level accuracy: RIG = λ RG + (1 λ) R(or), (21) where λ [0, 1] controls the tradeoff between variance reduction and sample-level discrimination. Accordingly, we define the confidence reward as Rc(oc) = confidence(oc) RIG . (22) To ensure structural compliance, we apply penalty when the model fails to follow the prescribed output format. The resulting accuracy and confidence advantage are defined as: Ar,i = R(or,i) mr σr , Ac,i = Rc(oc,i) mc σc , (23) where mr, mc and σr, σc denote the mean and standard deviation of corresponding rewards within the group. 5.3. Masked Gradient Optimization To prevent interference between correctness optimization and confidence calibration, we adopt masked gradient optimization strategy that applies distinct advantage signals to different token blocks. For each response oi, we construct token-level mask that separates reasoning tokens or from confidence tokens oc. During policy optimization, advantages signals are applied exclusively to their corresponding token subsets: 1 G (cid:88) i=1 1 oi (cid:88) yj or ˆρi,j Ar,i + (cid:88) yj oc ˆρi,j Ac,i , (24) where ˆρi,j denotes the clipped importance sampling ratio for token yj. This block-wise masked optimization ensures that gradients derived from correctness supervision do not affect confidence estimation, and vice versa, enabling effective decoupling of reasoning accuracy and calibration objectives under shared policy. Theorem 5.1 (Statistical Optimality of Decoupled Calibration). Under strictly proper scoring rule, the optimal confidence predictor satisfies E[c q] = Eyπθ (q)[R(y)]. (25) Proof. Consider R(y) as i.i.d Bernoulli distribution. For proper calibration estimation function ℓ(c, r), the expected loss should be minimized at = r, and the expected loss arg min ErBern(p)[l(c, r)] = p. (26) Since πθ(cq, y) is optimized independly of reasoning policy πθ(yq), the expectation loss E[cq] converges without affect πθ(yq) explicitly. Thus, E[cq] = Eyπθ(q)[R(y)]. (27) The detailed proof is provided in Appendix A.3. Theorem 5.1 establishes that decoupled confidence estimation yields statistically consistent uncertainty estimates without interfering with policy optimization. 6. Experiments We conduct extensive experiments on mathematical tasks, demonstrating that our proposed method improves confidence calibration while preserving reasoning accuracy. 6.1. Experimental Settings Datasets. All reinforcement learning methods are trained on the DeepScaler dataset. For evaluation, we use suite of mathematical reasoning benchmarks with increasing difficulty, including MATH-500, AIME 2024/2025 and AMC 2023/2024, which enable joint assessment of reasoning accuracy and confidence calibration. Baselines. We compare DCPO with representative methods spanning standard RL, post-hoc calibration, and calibrationaware RL: 1) GRPO (Shao et al., 2024): the standard GRPO objective, serving as the primary baseline for reasoning performance. 2) ConfClass: post-hoc MLP confidence predictor trained on token-level generation statistics; 3) RLCR (Damani et al., 2025): which incorporates Brier Score calibration loss into the reward; 4) CCGSPG (Liu et al., 2025): which modifies the GRPO objective according to token-based confidence. All methods are implemented on widely used LLM Qwen3-8B (non-thinking) to ensure 7 Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards Table 2. Ablation results averaged over 5 mathematical benchmarks. Variant DCPO w/o Instance-level Labels w/o Group-level Labels w/o Decoupled Optimzation w/o On-policy Training Acc ECE PECE 60.8 60.5 58.7 57.3 56.3 0.128 0.209 0.138 0.258 0.223 0.126 0.229 0.122 0.247 0. dynamics: GRPO exhibits increasing over-confidence during RL, with PCE rising from 0.483 to 0.556 on AIME25, whereas DCPO consistently suppresses over-confidence while maintaining stable accuracy. These results demonstrate that DCPO effectively mitigates RL-induced overconfidence and achieves substantially better accuracycalibration tradeoff. 6.3. Ablation Studies We conduct ablation studies to quantify the contribution of each key component in DCPO. Table 2 reports the averaged accuracy and calibration performance across five mathematical benchmarks. Effectiveness of decoupled optimization. Removing decoupled optimization causes severe degradation across all variants, in particular, ECE increases sharply from 0.128 to 0.258, while accuracy drops from 60.8% to 57.3%, which indicates strong gradient interference in coupled accuracy and calibration optimization. In contrast, DCPO results in substantially better accuracy-calibration tradeoff. These results confirm that decoupling is essential for stabilizing training and preventing calibration objectives from harming reasoning performance. Effectiveness of hybrid group-instance supervision. As shown in Table 2, removing instance-level labels leads to notable increase in ECE from 0.128 to 0.209, while removing group-level labels causes clear accuracy drop from 60.8% to 58.7%, indicating that group-level accuracy provides low-variance supervision signal that stabilizes optimization, whereas instance-level correctness enables finer-grained confidence differentiation. By jointly leveraging both signals, DCPO achieves the lowest ECE (0.128) while maintaining the highest accuracy (60.8%), demonstrating the effectiveness of hybrid calibration supervision. Importance of on-policy calibration. As shown in Table 2, off-policy calibration reduces accuracy from 60.8% to 56.3% and increases ECE to 0.223, which demonstrates that off-policy training can interfere with previously learned reasoning behaviors. In contrast, DCPO preserves reasoning performance while achieving better calibration. Overall, these results demonstrate that decoupled optimizaFigure 6. Accuracy and PCE on AIME25 dataset at different training steps for GRPO and DCPO. The figures illustrate that during the training process, our method can significantly reduce over-confidence while preserving accuracy. fair comparison. Evaluation Metrics. We evaluate models from 3 complementary perspectives: we use accuracy for reasoning performance, ECE and AUROC for calibration evaluation, and PCE to assess over-confidence.. Hyperparameters All models are trained with global batch size of 256, group rollout size 8, for 5 epochs (approximately 120 steps). Evaluation is conducted with temperature of 0.7, top-p 0.8, and top-k 20, following the official Qwen3-8B evaluation protocol (Yang et al., 2025). Detailed training and evaluation parameters are in Appendix B. 6.2. Overall Results Table 1 reports reasoning accuracy together with multiple calibration metrics. These results demonstrate that decoupling correctness and confidence optimization is critical for achieving well-calibrated confidence without degrading reasoning ability. Coupled optimization methods suffer from an accuracycalibration tradeoff. As shown in Table 1, RLCR reduces PCE on AIME24 from 0.510 (GRPO) to 0.214, but at the cost of substantial accuracy drop from 40.0% to 32.8%. similar trend is observed for CCGSPG, which indicates that directly incorporating calibration losses into the RL objective interferes with correctness-driven policy optimization. Post-hoc calibration is insufficient for correcting miscalibration. As shown in Table 1, adding classifier on top of GRPO yields only marginal ECE improvements (e.g., from 0.370 to 0.363 on AIME24), while achieving low AUROC of 0.642, substantially below DCPOs 0.914, which indicates that RLVR significantly distorts the models internal representation, making post-hoc methods ineffective at substantially reducing mis-calibration. DCPO achieves favorable accuracy-calibration tradeoff and reduces over-confidence. In contrast, DCPO jointly improves calibration and preserves reasoning performance. For example, on AIME24, DCPO achieves 41.6% accuracy, which is comparable to GRPO while reducing PCE from 0.505 to 0.212. Figure 6 further illustrates the training 8 Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards hibit heavily skewed confidence distributions, reflecting severe over-confidence. RLCR collapses confidence estimates toward extreme values, indicating poor confidence granularity. In comparison, DCPO produces substantially balanced and continuous confidence distribution, which demonstrates that decoupled calibration with hybrid supervision is critical for learning expressive and reliable verbalized confidence. 7. Conclusion In this paper, we analyze in theory that RLVR inherently induces over-confidence. Moreover, we identified gradient conflict between accura and calibration optimization. Based on these insights, we propose DCPO, decoupled confidence-aware policy optimization framework. Experiments demonstrate that DCPO significantly improves calibration while preserving reasoning performance. Our study highlights the importance of decoupled optimization in model calibration.

sections.title: Impact Statement
sections.content: This paper presents work whose goal is to advance the field of Machine Learning. There are many potential societal consequences of our work, none of which we feel must be specifically highlighted here.

Detailed Analysis DCPO enables stable optimization dynamics. To analyze how different calibration strategies affect optimization behavior, we track the ℓ2 norm of policy gradients in training. Figure 7 compares the gradient norm trajectories of GRPO, RLCR, DCPO-I, and DCPO. In particular, instance-level calibration methods, such as RLCR and DCPO-I, exhibit pronounced gradient norm fluctuations with frequent spikes across training steps, indicating high-variance and unstable optimization. In contrast, DCPO maintains markedly smoother and more stable gradient norm profile throughout training, which demonstrates that DCPO achieves more stable optimization dynamics. DCPO introduces balanced confidence distribution. To analyze how different training strategies shape verbalized confidence behavior, we visualize the distribution of predicted confidence across benchmarks in Figure 8. Suppose by contradiction that there exists / + with p(y) > 0. Then by complementary slackness, µy = 0, and from (28) we obtain λ = 0, implying λ = 0. However, for any +, (28) then yields 1 λ + µy = 1 + µy > 0, which contradicts stationarity. Hence, p(y) = 0 for all / +. Next, consider +. If p(y) > 0, then by complementary slackness µy = 0, and (28) implies λ = 1. Therefore, for all +, the KKT conditions are satisfied whenever p(y) > 0. This shows that any probability distribution supported entirely on + satisfies the KKT conditions and hence achieves the same optimal objective value. However, such solutions form face of the simplex (Y) whose extreme points are precisely the Dirac measures δy for +. Since any distribution with support size greater than one can be written as non-trivial convex combination of other feasible points, it is not an extreme point of the feasible set. Consequently, any optimal solution attained at an extreme point assigns probability mass 1 to single trajectory +. This proof result holds irrespective of the size or structure of +, and can easily extend to expective-reward objectives with bounded linearity rewards. 12 Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards We further note that such low-entropy solutions are stable under small perturbations of the input. Let [0, 1] denote the predicted confidence, and let l(c, r) be strictly proper scoring rule. Then the minimizer of satisfies Eyπθ(x)Ecπθ(x,y)[l(c, R(y))] E[cx] = Eyπθ(x)[R(y)] Proof. For fixed x, with policy πθ(yx), the correctness reward R(y) is Bernouli distribution with mean = Eyπθ [R(y)]. For proper calibration estimation function l(c, r) which reaches its minimum at = r, the expected loss ERBern(p)[l(c, R)] is uniquely minimized at = p. Since πθ(cx, y) is optimized independenty of the generation policy πθ(yx), the expected confidence E[cx] converges to the unique minimizer without affect πθ(yx) explicitly. Therefore, E[cx] = Eyπθ [R(y)]. Decoupled confidence estimation yields statistically consistent calibration without altering the optimality conditions of the generation policy. A.4. sections.title: Value
sections.content: Qwen3-8B 1024 3000 5 1 106 256 0 8 1.0 0.20 0.28 Table 3. Key hyperparameters used in reinforcement learning training. B. Training and Evaluation Details B.1. Training Setup All reinforcement learning experiments are conducted using the VERL framework on cluster of 8 NVIDIA A100 (80GB) GPUs. Table 3 summarizes the key hyperparameters used throughout our experiments. For GRPO and CCGSPG, models are trained directly on the original DeepScaler dataset. For methods requiring explicit confidence supervision (e.g., RLCR and DCPO), prompts are modified to instruct the model to output confidence score following its final answer. The trade-off hyperparameter λ in DCPO is set to 0.5. Post-training calibration methods are initialized from the GRPO checkpoint at step 120 and further trained for an additional 80 steps using only Brier Score calibration rewards. B.2. Confidence Estimation Baseline For the ConfClass baseline, confidence is predicted using three-layer MLP with hidden dimension 2048. Training data are generated by sampling outputs from the GRPO-120 checkpoint on the DeepScaler dataset over 20 independent runs. Each sample includes the top-20 token log-probabilities as input features, with the corresponding group-level average accuracy as supervision. The model is trained using cross-entropy loss with learning rate of 1 104. B.3. Evaluation Protocol Unless otherwise specified, all results are obtained from the 120-step checkpoint of each training run. Confidence is extracted in two ways: (logits): computed as the sequence generation probability by aggregating token-level log-probabilities during decoding. (verbal): extracted from model outputs explicitly prompted to provide confidence score following the <conf> tag. During evaluation, responses are sampled using temperature 0.7, top-p 0.8, and top-k 20 (Yang et al., 2025). To reduce variance on smaller evaluation sets (e.g., AIME24, AMC23), each evaluation is repeated four times with independent sampling, and results are averaged. Task accuracy is computed using the OpenCompass framework. Calibration metrics, including Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Brier Score (BS), are calculated directly from confidenceaccuracy pairs. AUROC is computed using the standard scikit-learn implementation. 16 Decoupling Reasoning and Confidence: Resurrecting Calibration in Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards B.4. Verbalized Confidence Prompts To enable explicit confidence prediction, training and evaluation prompts are augmented with instructions requiring floating-point confidence output between 0 and 1. The prompt format is as follows: Please put your final answer within boxed{}. Also output your confidence for this answer after <conf> as floating-point number between 0 (complete uncertainty) and 1 (full confidence). Example: <conf> Confidence: 0.83 Ensure that the confidence output appears on single line and strictly follows the specified format.
